sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content: [{"factor": "Tobacco smoking", "type": "Behavioral", "detail": "The most established risk factor, accounting for approximately 50% of cases in men and 30% in women. Risk increases with duration and intensity of smoking and persists for years after cessation.", "strength": "Strong; cigarettes are the primary source, but pipe, cigar, and pipe smoking also increase risk."}, {"factor": "Occupational and environmental exposures", "type": "Environmental", "detail": "Exposure to aromatic amines (e.g., 2-naphthylamine, 4-aminobiphenyl), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and chlorinated solvents (e.g., trichloroethylene) in industries such as dye, rubber, leather, painting, and metalworking.", "strength": "Moderate to strong; well-established in occupational settings."}, {"factor": "Pelvic radiation therapy", "type": "Therapeutic", "detail": "Prior radiation therapy for cancers of the cervix, prostate, or other pelvic malignancies increases the risk of bladder cancer, typically after a latent period of several years.", "strength": "Moderate; risk is dose-dependent."}, {"factor": "Certain medications", "type": "Pharmacological", "detail": "Chronic use of cyclophosphamide (an alkylating agent) and pioglitazone (a thiazolidinedione diabetes medication) has been associated with increased risk.", "strength": "Moderate; based on epidemiological studies."}, {"factor": "Chronic urinary tract irritation or infection", "type": "Inflammatory/Infectious", "detail": "Long-term indwelling catheters, bladder stones, and chronic urinary tract infections are risk factors. Schistosoma haematobium infection is a major cause of squamous cell carcinoma in endemic regions.", "strength": "Moderate; particularly strong for squamous cell carcinoma in schistosomiasis-endemic areas."}, {"factor": "Personal or family history of bladder cancer", "type": "Genetic/Familial", "detail": "A personal history of bladder cancer increases the risk of recurrence and new tumors. First-degree relatives of patients with bladder cancer have a modestly increased risk, suggesting a hereditary component.", "strength": "Moderate; family history may indicate shared genetic or environmental exposures."}, {"factor": "Lynch syndrome", "type": "Genetic", "detail": "An autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), leading to a significantly increased risk of urothelial carcinoma, particularly of the upper tract.", "strength": "Strong; lifetime risk of urothelial carcinoma is up to 8%."}, {"factor": "Male sex", "type": "Demographic", "detail": "Men have a 3–4 fold higher incidence than women, which may be related to differences in smoking prevalence, occupational exposures, and hormonal factors.", "strength": "Strong; consistent across populations."}, {"factor": "White race", "type": "Demographic", "detail": "White individuals have a higher incidence compared to Black individuals in the United States, although Black patients present with more advanced disease.", "strength": "Moderate; incidence differences may be influenced by socioeconomic and environmental factors."}, {"factor": "Obesity", "type": "Metabolic", "detail": "Obesity is associated with a modestly increased risk of bladder cancer, potentially mediated by chronic inflammation and hormonal dysregulation.", "strength": "Moderate; meta-analyses suggest a positive association."}, {"factor": "Diabetes mellitus", "type": "Metabolic", "detail": "Type 2 diabetes mellitus is associated with an increased risk of bladder cancer, possibly due to hyperinsulinemia or medications used to treat diabetes.", "strength": "Moderate; evidence from meta-analyses is consistent."}]

cards.detail: The most established risk factor, accounting for approximately 50% of cases in men and 30% in women. Risk increases with duration and intensity of smoking and persists for years after cessation.

Exposure to aromatic amines (e.g., 2-naphthylamine, 4-aminobiphenyl), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and chlorinated solvents (e.g., trichloroethylene) in industries such as dye, rubber, leather, painting, and metalworking.
Prior radiation therapy for cancers of the cervix, prostate, or other pelvic malignancies increases the risk of bladder cancer, typically after a latent period of several years.
Chronic use of cyclophosphamide (an alkylating agent) and pioglitazone (a thiazolidinedione diabetes medication) has been associated with increased risk.

cards.factor: Chronic urinary tract irritation or infection
cards.detail: Long-term indwelling catheters, bladder stones, and chronic urinary tract infections are risk factors. Schistosoma haematobium infection is a major cause of squamous cell carcinoma in endemic regions.

cards.factor: Personal or family history of bladder cancer
cards.detail: A personal history of bladder cancer increases the risk of recurrence and new tumors. First-degree relatives of patients with bladder cancer have a modestly increased risk, suggesting a hereditary component.

An autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), leading to a significantly increased risk of urothelial carcinoma, particularly of the upper tract.
Men have a 3–4 fold higher incidence than women, which may be related to differences in smoking prevalence, occupational exposures, and hormonal factors.
White individuals have a higher incidence compared to Black individuals in the United States, although Black patients present with more advanced disease.
Obesity is associated with a modestly increased risk of bladder cancer, potentially mediated by chronic inflammation and hormonal dysregulation.
Type 2 diabetes mellitus is associated with an increased risk of bladder cancer, possibly due to hyperinsulinemia or medications used to treat diabetes.
